channel.title: One Square Meter
channel.description: A year-long biodiversity documetation of 1m² area in Gundmi, India.

item.title: Sighting: Hover Fly
item.description:
Hover Fly (Paragus crenulatus)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

I like these hover flies. Quite a pleasure to click some decent photos of these tiny fies. Their ability to just hover isteady is quite a charm.

Weather: 29.7°C, Overcast

item.title: Week 12: life outside the square
item.description:
The summer heat gets to you.

Hello!

The ghost ants (Tapinoma melanocephalum) have taken over the square meter plot. They are everywhere. They are tiny, barely visible and they move really fast. I think this is the reason the acrobat ants have moved out!

Life outside the square meter is still a bit hectic. I couldn't spend more than 4-5 minutes at a time to observe the plot.

But spending time with this hoverfly was fun.

You guessed right, it can hover in the air!

And They seem friendly and aren't easily spooked by my presence.

Also, I haven't seen the usual spiders (Lynx spiders, jumping spiders) in a while. Hah.

’Til next week!

item.title: Sighting: Chalcidid Wasp
item.description:
Chalcidid Wasp (Antrocephalus sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Looked quite distinct from the usual wasps. As soon as I saw the pic and noticed its thick legs, I knew this is Chalcidid wasp!

Weather: 29.4°C, Slight rain showers

item.title: Sighting: Knot-horn Moth
item.description:
Knot-horn Moth (Epicrocis sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

These small moths are so hard to photograph. They hug the grass blade upside down and there is barely any space to get a clear view!

Weather: 29.0°C, Slight rain showers

item.title: Sighting: Skin Beetle
item.description:
Skin Beetle (Attagenini sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

This one seemed distinct from earlier skin beetles I have logged.

Weather: 29.6°C, Overcast

item.title: Week 11: life outside the square
item.description:
Life exists outside the square meter too!

Hello!

The acrobat ants have disappeared overnight. I haven't spotted them in the last few days. I wonder what they are up to.

Otherwise, life outside the square meter got a bit hectic in the last few days, which involved a hospital stay as well. I did manage to spend a few minutes observing the plot every day and logged whatever bugs I could see. But this entire week, I haven't managed to spend quality time there. Hopefully, it will get better in the coming week.

’Til next week!

item.title: Sighting: Stink Bug
item.description:
Stink Bug (Acrosternum gramineum)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Stink bug! Lot of childhood memories of this one.

Weather: 29.2°C, Slight rain showers

item.title: Sighting: Chalcidoid Wasp
item.description:
Chalcidoid Wasp (Encyrtidae sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Tiny wasp, it was so windy I just couldn't take a sharp photo. Keeping this for record. May be I'll see this again soon. So hot these days. uff.

Weather: 29.2°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Velvet Ant
item.description:
Velvet Ant (Bischoffitilla sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Other day, I saw this running quite quickly across the plot. Barely could see the outline of it, and I remembered that it had spots. Today, it slowed enough for me to click couple of decent shots. Neat bug!

Weather: 28.8°C, Overcast

item.title: Week 10: Warm days
item.description:
Lack of bugs is information too

Hello!

Afternoon temperatures are hovering around 32–33 °C these days, and summer has only just begun. The Monsoon rains likely won’t arrive until mid-May.

I have barely observed any new species this week, the Three-striped Lady Beetle is a nice surprise.

The patch is drying up.

This might be the first weather-related change in insect density in the square meter patch. These days, it’s mostly ants.

I don’t know whether there are fewer bugs because it’s summer or because I’m paying less attention. I'll never know.

’Til next week!

item.title: Sighting: Leaf-Footed Bug
item.description:
Leaf-Footed Bug (Cletus trigonus)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

I like these bugs. Such a detailed patterns on them!

Weather: 28.7°C, Overcast

item.title: Sighting: Three-striped Lady Beetle
item.description:
Three-striped Lady Beetle (Brumoides suturalis)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Tiny beetle. Made camp at the square meter plot whole day today.

Weather: 28.7°C, Overcast

item.title: Sighting: Pygmy Dartlet
item.description:
Pygmy Dartlet (Agriocnemis pygmaea)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Such a nice little one. Happily sat still allowing me take all different angle photos.

Weather: 28.5°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Lynx Spider
item.description:
Lynx Spider (Oxyopes sertatus)

Category: Arachnid | Season: Summer

Biggest lynx spider yet!

Weather: 28.4°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Pea And Bean Weevil
item.description:
Pea And Bean Weevil (Bruchinae sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Summer

Tiny weevil. Looks like a black dot.

Weather: 28.6°C, Overcast

item.title: Sighting: Jumping Spider
item.description:
Jumping Spider (Carrhotus sp.)

Category: Arachnid | Season: Summer

Small spider. Jumps. Acutely aware that a camera is pointed at it!

Weather: 28.6°C, Overcast

item.title: Week 9: Struggle is real!
item.description:
Slow is smooth, smooth is good

Hello!

I crouch near the square meter plot, and notice all that moves. Carpenter ants, acrobat ants, few beetles, flies and wasps. All creating a constant sense of movement. They move with a sense of urgency and a goal.

Now I try practicing the art of "notice and let go" every time I spot a known bug. A temporary sense of familiarity kicks in. Now I am primed to notice unfamiliar things.

Every now and then, I spot an unfamiliar form moving about. My senses spring to action. I keep an eye on this bug as I move the camera and get ready to take a pic or two. Inevitably first few shots are shaky, unfocused and unusable.

I steady myself, hold my breath and let go of tension. I move along with the bug as I try to figure out what kind of bug it is. Is it moving in a predictable pattern? Can I anticipate where it will be in the next second? Or is it moving erratically that I have to resort to brute-forcing?

At this point, I am simply reacting to the movements of the bug. By now, I know this. Black Corinomma something spider. I have seen this before. It moves quite quickly and always takes the path through the grass and the undergrowth. Hoping it would hop onto a leaf and sitting still ain't gonna happen. I have to be really quick, steady and smooth.

Through the years of backyard wildlife photography, I kinda have an intuition if the shot I clicked is decent or not. I sense the images haven't come out well and a sense of urgency and frustration builds up, as the bug is moving near the border of the square meter plot.

Counter-intuitively, I have to slow down to take a decent shot rather than work myself up through this frustration.

The spider paused for ever so brief a moment. This is my chance. Perhaps the only chance I have today.

Click.

I think I got it. One more just to make sure I have it.

Here we go. Black Corinnomma Spider (Corinnomma severum).

I let the bug go on its way. I check the photos and I smile.

’Til next week!

item.title: Sighting: Spider Wasp
item.description:
Spider Wasp (Pompilidae sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

Found around 8 PM, couldn't find more details as it was quite dark. Not very confident about the ID. Wasps seem harder to narrow in on species.

Weather: 28.5°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Stinging Wasp
item.description:
Stinging Wasp (Aculeata sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

I spent one whole hour to narrow down this to a proper taxonomic level. Still not very sure. All AI seem to fail at this one.

Weather: 28.5°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Lady Beetle
item.description:
Lady Beetle (Scymninae sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

Almost ignored this moved on. Looked like a small dot.

Weather: 28.6°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Darth Maul Bug
item.description:
Darth Maul Bug (Spilostethus hospes)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

Mr. Maul is back. It is meditating.

Weather: 28.6°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Black Stink Bug
item.description:
Black Stink Bug (Coptosoma variegatum)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

This bug came back and it has wings! How cool!!

Weather: 28.6°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Satellite Fly
item.description:
Satellite Fly (Metopia sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

I moved a bit too fast and it flew away.

Weather: 28.6°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Sighting: Short-Horned Grasshopper
item.description:
Short-Horned Grasshopper (Acrididae sp.)

Category: Insect | Season: Winter

Small green thing.

Weather: 29.1°C, Partly cloudy

item.title: Week 8: Game of life (and death)
item.description:
Kill or be killed, that seems to be the vibe.

Hello!

Spiders. I find them very fascinating. Eight eyes, eight legs, venom, ability to jump, and they can make silky webs! Evolution surely optimized these critters alright.

I've been observing a particular type of spider named "Crab spider" from Thomisidae family. There are many of them here on the square meter plot. I see them regularly. It is hard to spot them if they aren't on the move.

They seem to really like to hunt the carpenter ants. On one particular night, I saw 4 of them having these ants for dinner.

I made a short video explaining what I learnt. Go, watch it!

’Til next week!

item.title: Week 7: It is complicated.
item.description:
It is nice to see a bug that I hadn't seen in a while.

Hello!

Aphids. These tiny, plumpy bugs (~ 1 mm in length) are at the center of a dramatic relationship between various ants, ladybugs and wasps.

It took me a good one month to even notice these tiny moving things! Now I see the drama around them everyday.

So, the aphids drink the plant's sap. But the sap is too sugary for aphids (I mean, no wonder they look so round!). So they secrete the excess sugar as "honeydew". So far, so good.

But sugar is like money of the insect world and everyone wants in.

The carpenter (and other types) ants "farm" these aphids for the sweet, sweet nectar. They kinda massage the aphids to "help" them secrete the honeydew. They move the aphids to juicy parts of the plant and even defend them from predators.

I’ve seen carpenter ants climbing up and down the grass all day to tend their aphids.

Interestingly, I noticed these red ants (Weaver ants I guess?) doing similar rounds as well!

Clearly, this is a profitable business.

But then again, more opportunistic insects thought why take the trouble for farming them, taking care of them when you can simply eat them? That's what the aptly named aphid wasps do. The other day I saw this aphid hunter running up and down the grass to select the best aphid and flying away with it!

Likewise, the ladybugs munch on these aphids too. I've seen them gobbling on these aphids all day long only to be interrupted by, you guessed it, the carpenter ants. Their face-off was quite underwhelming but photographing it wasn't easy.

Such a quiet drama, all day (and night) long and all within the square meter.

’Til next week!

item.title: Week 6: It feels like summer
item.description:
Somedays I see many bugs, other days very few.

Hello!

This square meter plot is drying and fast. The daytime temperature is rising, the Philippine Spinach has turned yellow.

I saw fewer bugs in the last few days, I suspect the trend might continue into summer.

It has just been a few weeks, and the data of the species is already a bit messy. Double counting, mis-identified bugs, same bug logged with genus name as well as family name etc.

I will fix it in the coming weeks.

’Til next week!

item.title: Week 5: A mirror of life
item.description:
Staring into the eyes of a tiny spider is quite haunting.

Hello!

This square meter plot is a battleground of life. I see relentless pursuit of survival; each bug for its own. Brutal. Each adopted evolutionarily to be specialist in certain ways, to be efficient hunter. Poetic.

Well, a month went by, and here we are. I have already seen a lot. Courting, mating, caring, and killing.

Circle of life, eh?

’Til next week!

item.title: Week 4: Dust on the sensor
item.description:
I see bugs in my dreams now.

Hello!

The ant-mimicking spiders have been the highlight of this week(one, two and three). I saw three different varieties, and each time they fooled me for a few moments. There are incredible theories about how they evolved into such convincing mimics.

I stepped out at night a few times this week with a headlamp to observe the plot. Night photography is really hard without the right equipment, but spotting the paddy field frog was quite nice.

The plot’s undergrowth is drying up, and I can now more or less see the soil easily, unlike the last few weeks.

Another unexpected thing is that the plot is becoming an island of greenery because of all my movement around it. The immediate surrounding has become dry. I can’t think of a way to avoid this for now.

’Til next week!

item.title: Week 3: Settling in
item.description:
Oh it's a bug's life for me

Hello!

The flies from Tephritidae family have a funky dating process. Check it out here. I had to push myself a bit to record all of the footage over 3.5 hours. It was quite fascinating to see the whole thing happening.

Otherwise, the patch is still green, and quite a few varieties of bugs are still around. I think I have seen five or six different species that I haven't been able to get a decent picture of yet. So, fingers crossed!

’Til next week!
